VO_EN: The first thing that disappears is not a building.
No one asked what you thought about the war.
The glass of water on the kitchen counter shakes.
You feel it in your feet first.
You don't remember when you started.
You're a software engineer in Tehran.
You build payment apps for a company that can't process international transactions.
You debug a function at two in the morning because the workaround for the sanctions workaround broke again.
Your boss sends a thumbs-up emoji.
Your job exists inside a country the world decided to cut off.
You just write code inside it.
Tehran is nine million people stuck on the Hemmat Expressway.
University students smoke in front of the bookshop on Enghelab Street.
Your coworker is angry because Persepolis lost again.
He's been angry about it all week.
He brings it up in every meeting.
That's the kind of argument you're allowed to have.
The barista at your corner cafe misspells your name on the cup.
Saffron ice cream from the shop on Valiasr every Friday.
You carry it home in a plastic bag that always drips.
The bag sticks to your fingers.
The cat doesn't look like a cat.
She tells you it's a cat.
She tapes it to the fridge door with masking tape that's already peeling.
Like weather you plan around but can't change.
You've learned where the lines are.
Insulin costs three months of salary.
The pharmacy has gaps on the shelves where the imported brands used to be.
You run your hand along the empty space.
The generic version comes from a factory that lost its supply chain two sanctions rounds ago.
The pill exists somewhere in the world.
The logistics don't exist in yours.
You check the dollar exchange rate every morning before breakfast.
There's nothing you can do about the number.
You thought these things would always be there.
The ice cream dripping down the bag on Fridays.
The argument about Persepolis that never mattered and always did.
By Wednesday your VPN icon spins and spins and never connects.
The screen on your phone becomes a mirror.
It shows your face and nothing else.
When a government kills the internet it's not hiding information from the outside.
It's building a wall around the inside.
Your mother calls from the neighbor's landline.
She walked three blocks in slippers.
She asks if you saw the news.
The first airstrike hits a military facility twelve kilometers from your apartment.
You feel the vibration in your feet before you hear anything.
The glass of water on the kitchen counter shakes.
In the dark you hear your neighbor praying through the wall.
The floor is cold under your feet.
If the next one is closer you move.
You don't remember the last time you prayed.
You just don't want to think about it.
You carry blankets and water and your daughter's insulin and your wife's documents and one phone charger down the stairs.
The plastic bag tears on the third landing.
A bottle of water rolls under a parked car.
Your daughter won't let go of the cat drawing.
She carries it folded in her fist the entire night.
The paper is soft by morning.
Twelve families in the parking garage.
One battery radio between all of them.
A man you've never spoken to gives your daughter his jacket.
You try to say thank you.
He waves it off before you finish.
Someone brings bread they baked that morning not knowing if there'd be a morning to bake it in.
Someone else brings a transistor radio that only picks up static and state news.
You try to explain it to someone once.
Over the phone when it works for ten minutes.
What it's like to sleep on concrete with twelve families you didn't know last week.
The words don't come out right.
After the strikes the checkpoints go up on Hemmat.
Men in uniforms patrol the neighborhoods.
A man in your building is taken because he has a satellite dish on his balcony.
He comes back two days later.
Says nothing about where he was.
Your office shares a block with something you didn't know was there.
An IRGC logistics center behind a concrete wall with no sign.
You walked past it every morning for three years.
Precision is a word that belongs to people who've never lived near the targets.
From twelve kilometers away it's a strategy. From your kitchen it's the glass of water shaking.
You're being bombed from above and controlled from within.
You're not sure when that started.
In the pharmacy there is one remaining box of insulin.
The man ahead of you says his son needs it.
You both know there won't be another shipment.
The pharmacist looks at both of you.
Your voice doesn't sound like yours.
The box is lighter than you expected.
He is still standing at the counter.
The cat drawing is still in her fist.
You're a person who ran out of choices the same day the pharmacy ran out of boxes.
You press them flat against your knees.
When the borders close the price of a bus seat to Turkey triples overnight.
A colleague at work sold his apartment for a third of its value just to get his family across.
Your wife says she won't leave without you.
You say you won't leave without your mother.
Your mother says she's too old to go.
The math is always one person short.
The bread queue starts at five in the morning.
By seven it wraps around the block.
Faces lit by phone screens checking for signal that won't come.
A woman ahead of you has two children hanging on her arms.
Everyone just walks home with empty bags.
No one checks their phone anymore.
When the internet comes back for thirty minutes your cousin in Canada calls.
She says the whole world is watching.
You want to say: you're not watching.
It doesn't watch the bread queue.
It doesn't watch the man at the pharmacy counter.
It doesn't watch twelve families sleeping on concrete.
But the connection cuts and you only have time to say: we're alive.
Your daughter is sitting on the floor.
She thinks planes belong to someone the way toys belong to someone.
Someone decided this in rooms you'll never enter in countries where children don't flinch at loud sounds.
You say: go draw the cat.
She draws the cat inside a box.
You don't ask what the box is.
When the ceasefire comes nothing returns.
The apartment has a crack running from ceiling to floor.
You put your finger on it once.
The metro station nearest your office is closed for structural damage.
You take a different route now.
The rial fell further than you can calculate.
The saffron ice cream shop on Valiasr is closed.
The owner left and didn't come back.
The cafe where the barista misspelled your name is still open.
You don't argue about football anymore.
You don't know when you stopped.
You just don't think about it.
Your daughter flinches at car doors slamming.
Your wife sleeps with the documents bag touching her arm.
Your mother says God is merciful.
She just checks the documents bag.
The cat drawing is still on the fridge.
The crack in the wall runs right through it.
Your daughter sits at the table.
You almost ask what the box is.
That is the only number remaining.
